w:r.w:t: Complete action plan for Claude Opus 4.6 — 23 ordered steps across 6 phases
How to read and use this document
Each step in this document follows the same structure. Read this once before starting.
Shows: step number | title | estimated effort | priority (CRITICAL / HIGH / MEDIUM / LOW)
Explains exactly what is broken or missing and why it matters for the product
The complete replacement file or code block — copy this exactly, replacing the existing file
The precise file path in the repo that you are creating or replacing
The exact command or check to confirm the step worked before moving to the next step
Do not skip steps. Steps 1–3 are blockers — the app will not build or run correctly until they are done.
Do not reorder steps within a phase. Dependencies run downward.
After each step, run the VERIFY command before continuing.
PHASE 0 — Fix the build: TypeScript path errors and missing env file (Steps 1–3)

The app currently does not build. ts_error3.log shows 10 TypeScript TS2307 errors — all caused by the same root cause: the @/* path alias in tsconfig.json only maps to ./src/*, but all lib/ imports use relative paths like ../../../../lib/. Next.js cannot resolve these at build time. Fix this before anything else.
tsconfig.json only defines @/* -> ./src/*. Every file inside src/app/api/ imports from lib/ using long relative paths like ../../../../lib/ledger/signer. At 4-5 directory levels deep these break.
Adding @lib/* -> ./lib/* lets every API route import as @lib/ledger/signer instead of counting ../../../.. This is the single fix that eliminates all 10 TS2307 errors in ts_error3.log.
tsconfig.json (repo root — replace the entire file)
After saving tsconfig.json, update every import in src/app/api/ that uses a relative ../../../ path to lib/.
Affected files: certify/route.ts, provenance/route.ts, verify/[id]/route.ts, provenance/[id]/route.ts, verify/pubkey/route.ts, enforce/tool-call/route.ts (6 files total)
Expected output: no errors. If errors remain, check the import path was updated in every affected file.
Create .env.local.example (missing file — blocks all developer onboarding)

The README says 'Create a .env.local file based on .env.local.example' but .env.local.example does not exist in the repo. Any developer cloning the project has no idea what variables to set. The app will silently fail with 'Missing Supabase environment variables' warnings.
This is also required by the original build spec.
# Copy this file to .env.local and fill in all values.
# ── Vercel cron auth (add to vercel.json crons) ───────────────────
Confirm the file appears in the repo root with ls .env.local.example
Confirm .env.local is listed in .gitignore (it already is — confirmed)
Fix hardcoded tenant ID in agents/register/route.ts

This means every agent registered through the UI goes to a fake hardcoded tenant, bypassing all JWT authentication. Any user calling this endpoint registers agents under someone else's tenant. This is the most serious security bug in the codebase.
The correct tenant ID is already available from the JWT middleware — it is injected into the X-Tenant-Id request header automatically.
// Tenant ID comes from JWT middleware — never hardcode this
const { name, type, framework, scopes } = body;
return NextResponse.json({ error: 'Missing required fields: name, type' }, { status: 400 });
return NextResponse.json({ success: true, agent: result.agent, token: result.token });
POST /api/v1/agents/register without a Bearer token → should return 401 Unauthorized
POST /api/v1/agents/register with a valid tenant JWT → should return 200 with agent and token
Check Supabase dashboard: the created agent_credentials row should have the correct tenant_id from the JWT, not '11111111...'
PHASE 1 — Wire real LLM calls into the SDK (Steps 4–6)

The SDK is the primary customer integration point. Currently ATPClient.chat() returns a hardcoded mock string and never calls OpenAI or Anthropic. A pilot customer integrating this SDK would certify fake outputs. These steps make the SDK functional.
Install OpenAI package and wire real call in ATPClient
lib/sdk/atp-sdk.ts line 28: const mockUpstreamResponse = 'Simulated AI response for testing S3 provenance.'

The SDK never calls any real LLM. Every certificate in the ledger today is certifying a fake string. The first thing a pilot customer will discover is that their actual model outputs are not being certified.
This is the Week 5 exit criteria from the build plan: 'first live certificate generated.'
Run in terminal: npm install openai (adds openai to package.json and node_modules)
import Anthropic from '@anthropic-ai/sdk'; // add: npm install @anthropic-ai/sdk
console.warn('ATP: Certification failed — returning uncertified response');
Create a test file test-sdk.mjs at repo root:
const client = new ATPClient({ provider:'openai', apiKey:process.env.OPENAI_API_KEY, atpKey:'test' })
const r = await client.chat({ model:'gpt-4o-mini', messages:[{role:'user',content:'Say hello'}] })
Expected: real OpenAI response text + a UUID certificate_id (not 'uncertified')
Fix model version fingerprinting — remove random anomaly simulation

This fires a false Slack alert and writes a fake audit event on 5% of all production calls. In a real deployment with 1000 certificates per day, that is 50 false alerts per day. This will destroy trust in the system before a customer even looks at it.
The fix: store real fingerprints in Supabase and compare on every call.
First, add this migration to Supabase. Run it in the SQL editor or via the Supabase CLI:
id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
tenant_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES tenants(id),
first_seen_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
last_seen_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
ALTER TABLE model_version_fingerprints ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;
* Generates a structural fingerprint from response characteristics.
* Uses: sentence count, avg word length, punctuation density,
* markdown presence, paragraph count — stable across minor variations.
// Fetch the last known fingerprint for this provider+model
// First time seeing this model — store baseline, no alert
// Only alert if fingerprint changed AND we have > 10 samples (avoids noise)
const eventDetails = { model_version: modelVersion, provider, model,
`⚠️ Model fingerprint changed: ${provider}/${model} — possible silent update`,
// Update stored fingerprint to new baseline
Make 15+ calls via ATPClient.chat() with the same model
Check Supabase → model_version_fingerprints table: one row per model with sample_count incrementing
No Slack alerts should fire for normal calls. Alerts only fire when fingerprint actually changes after 10+ samples.
Add agent-id validation in the certify route

src/app/api/v1/provenance/certify/route.ts accepts any string in the X-Agent-Id header without checking whether that agent exists or belongs to the tenant. A caller can certify outputs as 'agent_id=any-fake-uuid' and it gets stored unchallenged in the ledger.
This breaks the S6→S3 trust chain that is the platform's core defensibility.
return NextResponse.json({ error: 'Missing Tenant Authentication' }, { status: 401 });
// Validate agent belongs to this tenant (if provided)
return NextResponse.json({ error: 'Unknown agent_id for this tenant' }, { status: 403 });
const result = await CertificateService.certifyCall(tenantId, agentId || null, body);
// Async version fingerprint check — does not block response
POST /api/v1/provenance/certify with valid tenant JWT + no X-Agent-Id → 200 (agent is optional)
POST /api/v1/provenance/certify with valid tenant JWT + X-Agent-Id of a suspended agent → 403
POST /api/v1/provenance/certify with valid tenant JWT + fake agent UUID → 403
PHASE 2 — Complete S1 Conflict Arbiter: resource locking + intent release (Steps 7–9)

S1 currently only does semantic similarity conflict detection. It is missing exact resource locking (the $2M incident path) and the intent release mechanism. Without these, agents can deadlock each other via TTL expiry rather than clean release, and two agents writing to the exact same row in the same table will not conflict if they phrase their intents differently.
Add resource lock table and exact-match locking to S1

The spec's $2M logistics incident was not caused by semantically similar intents — it was caused by two agents acting on the exact same resource ('inventory:SKU-441') with different instructions ('reduce by 50' and 'reserve 50'). Cosine similarity between those sentences is only ~0.6 — below the 0.9 conflict threshold. They would not be caught.
Exact resource locking via a database lock table catches this regardless of how the intent is phrased.
id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
tenant_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES tenants(id),
agent_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES agent_credentials(id),
locked_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
UNIQUE(tenant_id, resource) -- Only one lock per resource per tenant
ALTER TABLE resource_locks ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;
CREATE POLICY rl_tenant ON resource_locks FOR ALL USING (tenant_id = auth.uid());
const reason = `Resource '${resource}' is locked by agent ${existingLock.agent_id}`;
suggested_action: 'Wait for the other agent to release the resource lock.' };
suggested_action: 'Modify intent to comply with corporate policy.' };
suggested_action: 'Wait for conflicting agent to complete or request HITL resolution.' };
// ── Step 4: Register intent + acquire resource lock ─────────
id: intentId, tenant_id: tenantId, agent_id: agentId,
await this.logArbiterEvent(tenantId, agentId, request.intent_description, 'allow', 'No conflicts detected.');
return { decision: 'allow', reason: 'Intent mediated and allowed.', intent_id: intentId };
static async releaseIntent(tenantId: string, intentId: string): Promise<void> {
tenantId: string, agentId: string, intent: string, decision: string, reason: string
tenant_id: tenantId, agent_id: agentId, request_id: uuidv4(),
Add intent release and override API endpoints to S1

Currently there is no way to release an intent lock. An agent that completes its action has no way to free the resource for other agents — they must wait for the TTL to expire. This makes S1 unusable in production for time-sensitive workflows.
Additionally, when S1 blocks an action, there is no human override path — a blocked agent is stuck forever.
src/app/api/v1/intent/route.ts (replace entire file — adds DELETE handler)
const result = await ArbiterService.mediateIntent(tenantId, agentId, body);
// DELETE: Release intent lock after action completes
if (!intentId) return NextResponse.json({ error: 'Missing intent_id' }, { status: 400 });
return NextResponse.json({ released: true, intent_id: intentId });
.select('id, agent_id, intent_description, status, metadata, expires_at, created_at, agent_credentials(agent_name)')
// PUT: Human override — unblock a blocked intent
const { intent_id, action, reason } = await request.json();
return NextResponse.json({ error: 'Missing intent_id or action' }, { status: 400 });
return NextResponse.json({ overridden: true, intent_id, action });
POST /api/v1/intent with resource:'inventory:SKU-441' from agent A → 200, intent_id returned
POST /api/v1/intent with resource:'inventory:SKU-441' from agent B → 403 (resource locked)
DELETE /api/v1/intent?intent_id=<id from step 1> from agent A → 200 released:true
POST /api/v1/intent with resource:'inventory:SKU-441' from agent B (again) → 200 (lock released)
Add configurable similarity thresholds per tenant
lib/modules/s1-conflict/policy-engine.ts — add getTenantThresholds() and update both methods to use it
// Add this method to PolicyEngine class in policy-engine.ts:
// Default thresholds — tenants can override via PUT /api/v1/policy/thresholds
// Store custom thresholds in a tenant_config JSONB column (add to tenants table)
// Update evaluatePolicy() to use: const { policyMatchThreshold } = await this.getTenantThresholds(tenantId);
// Update checkConcurrentConflicts() to use: const { conflictThreshold } = await this.getTenantThresholds(tenantId);
PHASE 3 — Complete S7 HITL: SLA enforcement, reviewer routing, training loop (Steps 10–13)

S7 currently creates and resolves tickets but has no automatic escalation (SLA timers never fire), no reviewer assignment logic, and no training pipeline feedback. These three gaps mean S7 is a ticket system, not a governance tool.

hitl_exceptions rows have a sla_deadline column but nothing checks it. An exception can sit open for days past its deadline with no escalation. The Vercel platform supports cron jobs via vercel.json — this is the correct serverless pattern instead of Celery.
vercel.json (replace entire file — adds cron config)
src/app/api/cron/sla-check/route.ts (create this new file and directory)
// Vercel calls this every 5 minutes. Protect with CRON_SECRET env var.
// Find all open exceptions past their SLA deadline
const { data: breached, error } = await supabase
Create a test exception with sla_deadline set to 1 minute in the past
Check Supabase: exception status should be 'escalated'
Check audit_events: hitl.sla_breached event should be present
Add training pipeline webhook to S7 resolve endpoint

The training pipeline feedback loop is what separates S7 from a basic ticketing system. When a reviewer approves or rejects an exception, that decision should be pushed to the customer's ML training pipeline (Databricks, SageMaker, etc.) so the model learns from human corrections.
This is the key defensibility feature: reviewer calibration data is proprietary and non-transferable.
id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
tenant_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES tenants(id) UNIQUE,
active BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT true,
created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW()
ALTER TABLE training_pipeline_webhooks ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;
CREATE POLICY tpw_tenant ON training_pipeline_webhooks FOR ALL USING (tenant_id = auth.uid());
lib/modules/s7-hitl/service.ts — update resolveException() to add training webhook call after audit log
// Add this block AFTER the audit log in resolveException(), before the Slack notify:
// Fire training pipeline webhook if configured
// Do not await — fire and forget, don't block the response
Use a webhook testing service like https://webhook.site to capture the payload
Configure the training_pipeline_webhooks URL via Supabase dashboard (or build the PUT /api/v1/training-pipelines route)
Resolve an exception and confirm the webhook fires with the correct payload shape
Add agent suspension endpoint (missing S6 route)
export async function POST(request: Request, { params }: { params: { id: string } }) {
// Verify agent belongs to this tenant
const { data: agent, error: fetchErr } = await supabase
return NextResponse.json({ error: 'Agent not found' }, { status: 404 });
return NextResponse.json({ error: 'Agent is already suspended' }, { status: 409 });
return NextResponse.json({ suspended: true, agent_id: agentId, reason });
middleware.ts line 29 has a comment: '// NOTE: A real Redis-backed rate limiter is needed for production here' with no implementation.
Without rate limiting, the /certify endpoint can be called unlimited times — a misconfigured SDK could drain a customer's Supabase quota and generate millions of fake certificates in minutes.
Use Upstash Redis (serverless, free tier at upstash.com) as the rate limit store.
// Add this block to middleware.ts AFTER the auth check, BEFORE return response:
limiter: Ratelimit.slidingWindow(1000, '1 m'), // 1000 req/min per tenant
// In the middleware function, after extracting tenantId:
{ error: 'Rate limit exceeded. Max 1000 requests/minute per tenant.' },
PHASE 4 — Build S5 Insurance state machine and compliance engine (Steps 14–17)

S5 currently stores insurance claims in a flat table with 4 statuses and no transition validation. The spec defines 8 states with enforced transitions, FCRA/NAIC compliance rules, and the requirement that S5 calls S3/S6/S7 internally rather than reading audit_events directly.
Add claim_state column and build state machine
ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS claim_state TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'fnol',
ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS fraud_score NUMERIC DEFAULT 0.0,
ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS compliance_flags JSONB DEFAULT '[]',
): Promise<{ success: boolean; claim?: any; error?: string }> {
return { success: false, error: `S6 permission denied: ${permCheck.reason}` };
const { data: claim, error: fetchErr } = await supabase
if (fetchErr || !claim) return { success: false, error: 'Claim not found' };
// 5. Certify the state transition via S3
tenant_id: tenantId, agent_id: agentId, request_id: uuidv4(),
payload: { claim_id: claimId, from: currentState, to: targetState, reason }
Add fraud scorer and FCRA/NAIC compliance rules
// Simple rule-based fraud scorer — slot for ML model later
if (daysSinceFiled < 1) score += 0.1; // Filed same day as incident — suspicious
// NAIC-002: Acknowledgement within 10 business days of FNOL
violations.push('NAIC-002: Acknowledgement letter overdue (10 business days)');
// NAIC-003: Accept/deny within 15 business days of triage
violations.push('NAIC-003: Decision deadline overdue (15 business days)');
// ATP-001: Claims > $50,000 require HITL before decided
violations.push('ATP-001: Claims > $50k must pass HITL before decision');
description: `Claim ID: ${claim.id}. Financial impact exceeds $50,000 threshold.`,
violations.push('ATP-002: High fraud score requires HITL review');
Update insurance API route to use state machine
src/app/api/v1/insurance/route.ts — update POST to use ClaimsStateMachine and computeFraudScore
// Replace the POST handler in insurance/route.ts with:
const { action, claim_id, incident_type, financial_impact, description } = body;
return NextResponse.json({ error: 'Missing incident_type or financial_impact' }, { status: 400 });
return NextResponse.json({ claim, message: 'Claim filed. State: FNOL.' });
tenantId, agentId, claim_id, body.target_state, body.reason || 'State transition'
return NextResponse.json({ claim: result.claim, transitioned_to: body.target_state });
// Uses mock mode when GUIDEWIRE_BASE_URL is not set.
// Real sandbox access provided during MGA pilot onboarding.
if (this.isMock) console.info('GuidewireClient: running in mock mode');
if (this.isMock) return { id: claimId, status: 'Open', claimant: 'Mock Claimant',
async updateClaimStatus(claimId: string, status: string, notes: string) {
if (this.isMock) return { updated: true, claimId, status };
private async request(path: string, method = 'GET', body?: any): Promise<any> {
PHASE 5 — Write the critical integration test (Steps 18–20)

Zero tests exist in the codebase. The build spec identifies the 5-event audit chain test as the most critical test — it must pass before any customer deployment. These steps add the testing infrastructure and write the required tests.
Install testing framework and configure for Next.js
Write the critical 5-event audit chain integration test

This is the test from the build spec: a single agent action must generate exactly 5 audit events in the correct order — S6 permission check, S1 intent registered, S3 certificate, S1 intent released, S3 response certificate.
If this test passes, all five modules are wired together correctly.
import { describe, it, expect, vi, beforeEach } from 'vitest';
// Mock Supabase so we test logic without hitting the DB
return { select: () => ({ single: () => ({ data, error: null }) }), error: null };
single: () => ({ data: { status: 'active', tenant_id: 'test-tenant', actions: ['write'], resource: 'inventory:SKU-441' }, error: null }),
delete: () => ({ lt: () => ({ data: null, error: null }), eq: () => ({ eq: () => ({ data: null, error: null }) }) }),
update: () => ({ eq: () => ({ data: null, error: null }) }),
// Mock signing so we don't need ATP_SIGNING_KEY in test env
it('single agent action produces correct audit chain', async () => {
// 2. S1: Register intent (+ acquire resource lock)
intent_description: 'Reduce inventory for SKU-441 by 50 units for flash sale',
// 3. S3: Certify the LLM call that produced the action
// 4. S1: Release intent lock after action completes
// S1 release updates DB, does not emit a new audit event (by design)
// 6. Verify modules logged to correct module identifiers
If any assertion fails, the module integration chain has a bug — fix it before proceeding
Write unit tests for S3 certificate hashing and S6 permission logic
it('produces stable SHA-256 hash for identical inputs', () => {
const input = JSON.stringify({ system: 'You are helpful', messages: [{ role:'user', content:'Hello' }] });
it('produces different hash when content changes', () => {
const input1 = JSON.stringify({ messages: [{ role:'user', content:'Hello' }] });
const input2 = JSON.stringify({ messages: [{ role:'user', content:'Goodbye' }] });
function checkPermission(scopes: {resource:string, actions:string[]}[], resource: string, action: string) {
PHASE 6 — Final polish: DEVIATIONS.md, priority ordering fix, metrics (Steps 21–23)
Create DEVIATIONS.md documenting the stack pivot

The build spec requires deviations from the spec to be documented in DEVIATIONS.md at the repo root. The stack change (FastAPI → Next.js, PostgreSQL standalone → Supabase, Docker → Vercel) is significant and must be documented for enterprise customers doing due diligence.
## DEV-001: Stack pivot from Python/FastAPI to TypeScript/Next.js
**Original spec**: Python FastAPI + PostgreSQL + Redis + Docker + Celery
**Actual implementation**: TypeScript Next.js 16 + Supabase + Vercel + Upstash Redis
**Rationale**: Faster time-to-demo, built-in Supabase auth, pgvector available
out-of-the-box, Vercel deployment eliminates DevOps overhead for early-stage.
**Functional parity maintained**: Ed25519 signing, immutable ledger rules,
RLS tenant isolation, pgvector similarity search, all API routes.
**Enterprise self-hosting**: Can be deployed on AWS/GCP/Azure as a container.
Contact: <founder email> for self-hosted deployment guide.
## DEV-002: Celery SLA timers replaced with Vercel cron jobs
**Original spec**: Celery task queue with Redis broker for SLA enforcement
**Actual**: Vercel cron job at /api/cron/sla-check running every 5 minutes
**Trade-off**: 5-minute granularity vs real-time. Acceptable for MVP SLAs.
## DEV-003: RS256 agent credentials deferred to post-MVP
**Original spec**: RS256 keypair per agent for credential signing
**Actual**: HS256 shared secret for MVP (all agents on same secret)
**Plan**: RS256 per-agent keypair generation added in v1.1 release.
## DEV-004: Guidewire integration runs in mock mode until MGA pilot
**Original spec**: Real Guidewire ClaimCenter REST API integration
**Actual**: Mock client with identical interface — real calls added at pilot.
Fix priority ordering in exceptions queue (alphabetical bug)
src/app/api/v1/exceptions/route.ts line 33 has a comment: '// Hackish: strict alphabetical ordering isn't perfect for enum but works for MVP'

Ordering by the 'priority' text column alphabetically gives: critical, high, low, medium — not the correct urgency order (critical > high > medium > low). This means medium-priority tickets appear after low-priority ones in the reviewer queue.
// Replace the query in GET /api/v1/exceptions with this version:
// Uses a CASE expression to enforce correct priority ordering
// Then sort in JavaScript by priority before returning:
const PRIORITY_ORDER = { critical: 0, high: 1, medium: 2, low: 3 };
const pa = PRIORITY_ORDER[a.priority as keyof typeof PRIORITY_ORDER] ?? 99;
const pb = PRIORITY_ORDER[b.priority as keyof typeof PRIORITY_ORDER] ?? 99;
return pa !== pb ? pa - pb : new Date(a.sla_deadline).getTime() - new Date(b.sla_deadline).getTime();
Add basic metrics endpoint for latency and throughput monitoring
// Returns operational metrics for Grafana or customer-facing SLA dashboard
const [certs, violations, exceptions, conflicts] = await Promise.all([
Completion checklist — verify all 23 steps before first customer demo
Fix hardcoded tenant ID in agents/register/route.ts
Wire real OpenAI/Anthropic calls in ATPClient.chat()
Fix VersionMonitor — remove Math.random(), add real fingerprinting
Add agent-id validation in certify route
Add resource_locks table and exact-match locking to S1
Add DELETE + PUT handlers to intent route (release + override)
Add configurable similarity thresholds to PolicyEngine
Add Vercel cron job for SLA enforcement (/api/cron/sla-check)
Add training pipeline webhook to S7 resolveException()
Add Upstash Redis rate limiting to middleware.ts
Add claim_state column and build ClaimsStateMachine
Add computeFraudScore() and FCRA/NAIC compliance rules
Update insurance route to use state machine
Install Vitest and configure for Next.js
Write S3 hashing and S6 permission unit tests
Fix priority ordering in exceptions queue
TRUSTLAYER — Step-by-Step Fix Instructions v1.0 — March 2026
Total estimated effort: ~28 hours — Critical blockers (Steps 1–3): 45 minutes
